data.title: Copart signals robust operational investments and storage expansion amid active storm season and shifting insurance cycles
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Copart, Inc. (CPRT) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jeff Liaw reported that "global insurance volume remained relatively flat year-over-year with a nominal decline of 0.3% globally in unit sales and 0.9% in the United States," but noted that "accounting for the extra business day of leap year 2024, global insurance and U.S. insurance units sold grew by 1.3% and 0.6%, respectively." Liaw emphasized that total loss frequency in the United States reached 22.8% in the first calendar quarter of 2025, up approximately 100 basis points from last year, driven by rising vehicle complexity and repair costs.
  • Liaw highlighted Copart’s preparation for an expected active 2025 storm season, referencing the acquisition of Hall Ranch in South Florida for nearly 400 usable acres of vehicle storage, which now enables Copart to “handle a storm more than 3 times the size of the largest Florida storms on record in Copart history.”
  • CFO Leah Stearns stated: “During the quarter, our global unit sales increased 1%, which reflects the modest headwind from the prior period being leap year. On a per business day basis, our global unit sales increased over 2%.” Stearns also described strong trends in non-insurance U.S. unit volume and a 14% year-over-year growth in Blue Car, which serves bank, rental, and fleet partners.

Outlook

  • Management indicated continued investments in physical storage, technology, and operational readiness for both storm response and daily business. Liaw explained that “our preparation is not an ad hoc spring event, but in fact a year-round exercise for us as a company.”
  • Stearns noted that secular trends in favor of rising total loss frequency are expected to drive long-term growth, while cyclical impacts associated with uninsured and underinsured motorists may influence near-term volumes.

Financial Results

  • Stearns reported that "global revenue increased to $1.2 billion." Global service revenue increased nearly $88 million or over 9% from the third quarter of 2024, and U.S. service revenue grew by 8%. International service revenue was up about 18%.
  • U.S. purchase vehicle revenue rose by about $20 million or 22%, while purchase vehicle gross profit decreased $13 million, or about 187%, which included a $12 million out-of-period adjustment related to prior quarters. International purchase vehicle revenue decreased over $23 million or 25%, but international purchase vehicle gross profit increased by over $2 million or about 22%.
  • Global gross profit was approximately $552 million, a $27 million increase, with a gross margin of 46%. U.S. gross profit was about $480 million with a 48% gross margin. International gross profit reached $73 million, with a 35% gross margin.
  • Third quarter GAAP operating income increased to approximately $452 million, and net income rose to $407 million or $0.42 per diluted share. Copart maintained liquidity of over $5.6 billion, including nearly $4.4 billion in cash.

Q&A

  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities, asked about the strategic value of Copart’s land assets for Blue Car and whole car customers. Jeff Liaw responded that physical storage is “essential to our value proposition for many sellers,” adding that “storage is increasingly difficult to procure anywhere in the United States, anywhere in any of the countries we serve.”
  • Labick inquired about the cyclical shift in insured motorists and its impact on volumes. Liaw stated, “We see cyclicality in it...around the time of the global financial crisis in 2008 and 2009 there was a local trough in insurance coverage. I think it’s not a surprise that we’re experiencing that again now.”
  • Craig Kennison, Baird, questioned Copart’s Purple Wave partnership and the effects of tariffs. Liaw explained that Purple Wave operates in an “uncertain environment, courtesy of the tariffs,” and described that tariffs have “created meaningful uncertainty for our clients, the insurance industry and otherwise.”
  • Chris Bottiglieri, BNP Paribas, asked about market share and auction price drivers. Liaw noted that “we’ve gained share...for many years,” attributing this to investments in land and technology. CFO Stearns added that the affordability gap in emerging markets continues to drive strong international buyer activity.
  • Jash Patwa, JPMorgan, asked about G&A spend trends and legislative impacts on storage fees and total loss thresholds. Stearns attributed G&A increases to salesforce investments at Purple Wave. Liaw discussed that legislation capping storage fees likely benefits insurance companies, while total loss threshold changes would not have a significant effect on Copart’s business model.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly cautious tone, probing on asset utilization, market share dynamics, effects of tariffs, and G&A run rates, seeking clarity on business resilience amid macro uncertainty.
  • Management’s tone was confident and focused on long-term secular trends, with Liaw stating, “We are excited to continue to invest our time and resources in growing and enhancing our capabilities.” During Q&A, management responded in detail, acknowledging cyclical headwinds but reiterating operational strengths and strategic investments.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained consistent, with ongoing attention to macro and regulatory headwinds. Management maintained a steady and confident tone, emphasizing continued investment and operational readiness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, with a continued focus on storage expansion, technology, and operational efficiency. Strategic focus slightly shifted towards readiness for an active storm season and navigating macro uncertainty in insurance coverage rates.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on macroeconomic cycles, G&A trends, and market share. In Q3, more attention was given to storm season preparedness and legislative developments.
  • Key metric changes include a modest increase in global unit sales and revenue, but a notable decrease in purchase vehicle gross profit due to adjustments. International gross margins improved despite revenue declines.
  • Management’s tone and confidence remained stable, emphasizing secular growth and operational investments. Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly cautious approach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited softness in the heavy equipment auction space, driven by uncertainty over infrastructure spending and tariffs.
  • There was mention of lower assignments, faster cycle times, and reduced low-value unit-aged inventory as key factors in declining inventory. Management’s mitigation includes ongoing investment in operational efficiency and digital platforms.
  • Legislative risks around storage fee caps and total loss thresholds were discussed, but management downplayed their potential to materially impact Copart’s core business model.

Final Takeaway

Copart’s third quarter 2025 results showcase continued investment in operational capacity, technology, and storage, positioning the company for both day-to-day strength and heightened storm season activity. Although global insurance volumes were flat, secular trends in total loss frequency and international demand underpin a resilient outlook. Management remains confident in its strategy, citing operational readiness, strong liquidity, and ongoing investments as key drivers for long-term growth, while addressing cyclical headwinds and legislative uncertainties with clear mitigation strategies.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: EPlus signals low single-digit net sales growth and mid-single-digit EBITDA increase amid services pivot and AI investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ePlus inc. (PLUS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mark Marron reported that "our financial results continue to reflect the ongoing industry wide shift towards ratable and subscription based revenue models," with higher gross profitability and margin expansion despite lower net sales and gross billings. Marron further highlighted, "operating metrics were strong with double digit increases in gross profit, operating income, adjusted EBITDA and diluted earnings per share."
  • Marron stated, "Q4 net sales declined 10.2% year-over-year, driven primarily by a reduction in product sales due in part to economic uncertainty as well as a tough compare to the prior year." He emphasized, "gross profit rose by nearly 12% and gross margin expanded 580 basis points year-over-year to 29.3% in the fourth quarter."
  • Services revenue continued to climb, with Marron citing a 33% increase for the quarter and 37% for the year. Managed services grew 16.6% for the quarter and 24.6% for the year.
  • The CEO underscored strategic investments in AI, cloud, security, and networking, and noted, "ePlus is the only NVIDIA partner in North America that has achieved both the NVIDIA DGX ready Super Pod specialization and DGX ready managed service provider specializations."
  • Security now represents 22% of gross billings on a trailing 12-month basis.
  • Marron described the year-end cash position as "a record cash position of approximately $389 million."
  • CFO Elaine Marion stated, "Consolidated net sales in the fiscal 2025 fourth quarter were $498.1 million, down from $554.5 million in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2024. And our consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $43.8 million, up from $36.8 million in the prior year, which exceeded our expectations as we captured increased gross profit from the sales of product."

Outlook

  • Marron introduced fiscal year 2026 guidance: "we are initiating fiscal year 2026 guidance for net sales growth of low single digits, while we expect gross profit and adjusted EBITDA to grow at mid-single digits over the prior fiscal year."
  • The guidance assumes some impact from economic uncertainty but does not factor in recessionary conditions or other unexpected developments.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales for Q4 were $498.1 million.
  • Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter was $43.8 million.
  • Technology business net sales declined 10.4% year-over-year to $487.2 million.
  • Service revenues increased 33% year-over-year, with professional services up 48% and managed services up 17%.
  • Fourth quarter consolidated gross profit increased 11.8% to $145.8 million, with gross margin rising to 29.3%.
  • Consolidated net earnings for the quarter rose 14.6% to $25.2 million or $0.95 per diluted share.
  • Full year consolidated net sales were $2.07 billion, down from $2.23 billion in fiscal 2024.
  • Full year consolidated gross profit rose 3.3% to $569.1 million, with gross margin expanding 270 basis points to 27.5%.
  • Cash position at the end of fiscal 2025 was $389.4 million.

Q&A

  • Greg Burns, Sidoti & Company, asked about the demand environment and product side softness. Marron responded, "We are seeing pickup in the data center, cloud and security space, that's not surprising... We did not see a pickup in networking year-over-year. So networking was down pretty big still for us."
  • Burns inquired about enterprise AI adoption and its impact on guidance. Marron answered, "Not yet, Greg, maybe towards the end of the year, beginning of next year... So we think a lot of that will be on the services side that will drive for us, which is a good thing, it's our most profitable business." He also noted, "We've made investments with the training of our sales teams. We also just got the NVIDIA Super Pod specialization, which is their high end computing. So over time, we think that infrastructure spend will pick up but it will take a little bit of time."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about ongoing product demand softness but showed interest in the growth areas of AI and managed services; the tone was neutral to slightly cautious.
  • Management’s prepared remarks conveyed confidence, especially highlighting margin expansion and the services pivot, while the Q&A responses were measured, acknowledging challenges in networking and product sales but expressing optimism for services and AI.
  • Compared with the previous quarter, management's tone appeared more confident about services-led growth, while analysts remained focused on demand softness and the timing of AI opportunity realization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from a revenue range of $2.07 billion to $2.11 billion in Q3 to low single-digit net sales growth for FY26, with mid-single-digit gross profit and EBITDA growth expected.
  • Management’s confidence in the services pivot increased, with a larger emphasis on AI capabilities and NVIDIA partnerships.
  • Analysts continued to press on product demand and AI adoption timing, similar to the previous quarter’s focus on product sales softness and netted down revenues.
  • Key financial metrics improved in gross margin and adjusted EBITDA versus the prior quarter.
  • Strategic priorities shifted further towards high-growth technology areas, subscriptions, and managed services.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited economic uncertainty, tariffs, and government spending as factors impacting sales.
  • Continued softness in networking and product demand was acknowledged as a headwind.
  • The company expects ongoing impact from the industry shift to ratable and subscription-based revenue models.
  • Analyst questions highlighted the risk of delayed AI infrastructure investment and the lag in networking recovery.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that ePlus is capitalizing on the accelerating industry shift to services, subscriptions, and high-growth technology segments, delivering strong margin expansion and profitability despite headwinds in product sales. Strategic investments in AI, cloud, and security are expected to drive future growth, supported by a robust balance sheet and a disciplined approach to capital allocation. The company projects low single-digit net sales growth and mid-single-digit gains in gross profit and adjusted EBITDA for fiscal 2026, reflecting confidence in the ongoing transformation of its business model and its ability to adapt to evolving customer needs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold price turns lower as dollar bounces higher, but debt concerns linger
data.description:
Gold futures turned lower Thursday after a three-day winning streak, as the dollar strengthened and investors locked in profits after prices touched a two-week high earlier in the session.

The U.S. dollar index rose 0.5%, rebounding after a steep slide since Moody's started the week by downgrading its U.S. credit rating, which exacerbated concerns over the U.S. debt burden and fiscal outlook, weighing on risk sentiment in recent days; a higher dollar makes bullion more expensive for foreign currency holders.

The U.S. bond market has been concerned about President Trump's "big beautiful" tax and spending bill, which finally passed the House of Representatives by a single vote and now moves on to the Senate; the legislation, as currently written, would add ~$3.8T to the federal government's $36.2B in debt over the next decade, according to the Congressional Budget Office, but includes an increase in the debt limit.

"We're seeing some profit taking pressure from recent gains and a firmer U.S. dollar index is another bearish factor," Kitco Metals analyst Jim Wycoff said in a note, but "the specter of a shaky global bond market is going to be a bullish underlying factor for the gold market that's going to limit the downside."

Meanwhile, traders continue to monitor developments in the Middle East, with another round of nuclear talks between the U.S. and Iran set to take place on Friday.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for May delivery ended -0.5% to $3,292.30/oz, and front-month May Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished -1.2% to $33.047/oz, snapping three-session winning streaks for both precious metals.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Ross Stores withdraws annual guidance amid tariff uncertainty, projects Q2 EPS range of $1.40–$1.55
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ross Stores (ROST) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Conroy emphasized the company’s resilience despite a volatile external environment, stating, "total sales grew 3% to $5 billion with comparable store sales flat versus last year. Earnings per share were $1.47 compared to $1.46 last year, while net income for the period was $479 million versus $488 million for the same period in 2024."
  • Conroy highlighted sequential monthly sales improvement, with cosmetics as the strongest merchandise area and the Southeast leading geographically. He noted, "our dd's DISCOUNTS brand continued its strong momentum from 2024 with another quarter of solid sales and operating profits."
  • Conroy addressed tariff impacts, saying the company will work to balance pricing and merchandise margin compression, adding, "it is possible that we will see short-term pressure on our profitability."
  • Inventory strategy was detailed with "total consolidated inventories were up 88% versus last year mainly due to opportunistic buys during the period. Average store inventories were up 4% in line with our plan and packaway merchandise represented 41% of total inventory."
  • Conroy announced the opening of 16 new Ross and 3 dd’s DISCOUNTS locations, continuing the plan for about 90 new stores this year, and stated, "there are simply too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility into the second half of the fiscal year and we believe it is prudent to withdraw our previously provided annual guidance at this time."
  • CFO Adam Orvos reported, "first quarter operating margin of 12.2% was similar to last year. Cost of goods sold was relatively unchanged from a year ago. Merchandise margin declined 45 basis points, mainly due to higher ocean freight costs and the initial impact of tariffs."
  • Orvos added, "during the first quarter, we repurchased 2 million shares of common stock for an aggregate cost of $263 million under the company's two-year $2.1 billion authorization."

Outlook

  • Orvos provided Q2 guidance: "comparable store sales are projected to be flat to up 3%. Earnings per share for the second quarter are now projected to be in the range of $1.40 to $1.55 and includes a cost impact of $0.11 to $0.16 from the announced tariffs."
  • Total sales for Q2 are forecast to increase 2% to 6% versus the prior year, with operating margin projected between 10.7% and 11.4%, including a 90 to 120 basis point negative tariff impact.
  • Management withdrew full-year guidance, citing "too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility into the second half of the fiscal year."

Financial Results

  • Sales and earnings performed at the high-end of company expectations for the quarter. Operating margin was flat at 12.2% year-over-year.
  • Merchandise margin was pressured by tariffs and ocean freight; occupancy and distribution costs rose modestly.
  • SG&A was flat year-over-year, as lower incentive compensation was offset by sales deleverage.
  • Inventory was notably higher due to opportunistic buying, with packaway merchandise steady as a share of inventory.
  • Share buyback activity continued, and the company remains on track for a $1.05 billion repurchase in 2025.

Q&A

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about drivers of sequential comp improvement and May trends. Conroy responded, "The sequential improvement was really broad-based across the merchandise hierarchy... we did guide to a flat to a plus three. So that should give you some sense of how we feel about the health of the business." Hartshorn explained tariff mitigation strategies, including vendor negotiations, careful pricing, and leveraging closeouts and packaway inventory.
  • Lorraine Hutchinson, Bank of America: Inquired about the gross margin hit from tariffs and whether impact may decrease. Hartshorn said, "Second quarter impact really includes... orders that were already in place when the tariffs were announced... we'll have to wait and see how the macro economy and retail environment evolves and what the outlook looks for inflation."
  • Mark Altschwager, Baird: Asked about inventory risk and strategic initiatives. Conroy noted, "in the short-term, I think there'll be availability of closeouts... we think we can get through that with no problem."
  • Tracy Kogan, Citigroup: Queried about tariff mitigation and customer trade down. Hartshorn said, "In the second quarter, it does include some mitigation," and saw broad-based comps across income bands.
  • Michael Binetti, Evercore ISI: Requested guidance range rationale and sourcing flexibility. Hartshorn explained the broader range was "out of abundance of caution given the macroeconomic and geopolitical environments."
  • Alexandra Straton, Morgan Stanley: Asked about branded strategy and women's apparel. Conroy stated, "we feel very good about the team's execution of the branded strategy... we don't expect margin headwinds going forward any longer from the brand strategy." Ladies business was slightly better than chain average.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on tariff impacts, inventory management, and the company’s visibility, with a generally neutral to slightly cautious tone, pressing for specifics on mitigation and strategic flexibility.
  • Management maintained a cautious, pragmatic tone, often reiterating uncertainty and highlighting multiple mitigation levers. Conroy stated, "there are simply too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility."
  • Compared to last quarter, the tone was more defensive, with management openly acknowledging lower visibility and the withdrawal of annual guidance as a prudent step.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from a previously provided annual outlook in Q4 to a withdrawal in Q1, signaling increased uncertainty due to tariffs and macroeconomic factors.
  • Management expanded discussion of tariff impacts and mitigation, which was only a minor topic last quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus moved from branded strategy and store productivity in Q4 to tariff risk, inventory strategy, and the flexibility of sourcing in Q1.
  • Management’s confidence in key initiatives, such as the branded strategy, remained, but overall tone reflected greater caution and less optimism regarding near-term trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated tariffs on Chinese imports, with management warning of "short-term pressure on our profitability."
  • Limited visibility on second-half performance due to consumer sentiment, inflation, and trade policy unpredictability.
  • Possible receipt risk from supply chain disruptions, though inventory is positioned to mitigate gaps.
  • Management cited broad-based inflationary pressure and acknowledged the potential for further impacts if macro conditions worsen.

Final Takeaway

Ross Stores delivered Q1 results at the high end of expectations despite a challenging environment, with improved sales momentum, solid inventory positioning, and strong execution of strategic initiatives such as the branded strategy. However, elevated tariff risk and macro uncertainty prompted the withdrawal of annual guidance, with management focusing on multiple mitigation levers and maintaining a cautious approach for the coming quarters. The outlook for Q2 projects comparable sales between flat and up 3%, and EPS in the $1.40 to $1.55 range, reflecting ongoing tariff impacts and a measured stance amid unpredictable trade and consumer conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump threatens to limit imports if countries don't pay more for drugs
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday threatened to limit imports of products from foreign countries if they don't work with pharmaceutical companies to pay more for drugs as a way to lower the costs for medicines in the United States.

Earlier this month, the president signed an Executive Order tying the prices of prescription drugs in the U.S. to what they cost in other countries under a program dubbed "Most Favored Nation."

If countries fight against drugmakers, "That's ok. We are not going to let you send any more cars into the United States," Trump said at a White House event for the release of the Make America Healthy Again report.

"Or we're not going to let you sell more wine or liquor or alcohol, or something that's actually much more important to them than the drugs. And we're going to be able to force that issue if we need to."

In his remarks, Trump predicted that Americans would save as much as 80% on prescription drugs compared to what they pay now with the Most Favored Nation program.

He also contended the MFN wouldn't impact pharmaceutical companies' bottom lines. "There shouldn't be a hit on their stock," adding that their income source will be "redistributed...so [other countries are] going to pay a little more, and we're going to pay a lot less."

data.title: MAHA report: 'Overmedicalization, poor diet' factors for childhood chronic diseases
data.description:
An assessment from the Make America Healthy Again Commission cites "overmedicalization, poor diet, aggregation of environmental chemicals, and lack of physical activity and chronic stress" as key reasons for the "childhood chronic disease crisis."

The report, mandated by President Trump through an Executive Action creating the panel, deals specifically with "the stark reality of children's declining health." It is titled "Make Our Children Healthy Again: Assessment."

HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy chairs the commission with Vincent Haley, Assistant to the President for Domestic Policy, as executive director.

"There is a concerning trend of overprescribing medications to
children, often driven by conflicts of interest in medical research, regulation, and practice," the assessment states regarding drugs. "This has led to unnecessary treatments and long-term health risks."

The report also targets ultra-processed foods, noting that about 70% of children's calories come from them, leading to obesity, diabetes, and other conditions. 

It also expresses concerns about children's exposure to synthetic chemicals, "some of which have been linked to developmental issues
and chronic disease."

High levels of inactivity, smartphone use, sleep deprivation, and chronic stress are contributing "to the rise in chronic diseases and mental health
challenges."

The document also targets childhood vaccine schedules, noting that the schedule in the U.S. has more shots than many European nations. "Despite the growth of the childhood vaccine schedule, there has been limited scientific inquiry into the links between vaccines and chronic disease, the impacts of vaccine injury, and conflicts of interest in the development of the vaccine schedule. These areas warrant future inquiry."

On Wednesday, The Wall Street Journal published an article detailing parts of the assessment.

By mid-August, the MAHA Commission is required to publish as strategy to address its findings.

data.title: Inomin Mines announces non-brokered private placement
data.description:
• Inomin Mines (TSXV:MINE:CA) announced on Thursday a non-brokered private placement of securities to raise total gross proceeds up to $200,000
  • The financing will consist of non-flow-through units (the "NFT Units") to be sold at a price of $0.035 per NFT Unit, said the company. 
  • "Each NFT unit will consist of one common share of the company and one common share purchase warrant. Each Warrant will entitle the holder thereof to purchase one additional share for a period of two years from the date of issuance at an exercise price of $0.05 per share," stated the company, adding that,  "a maximum of 10 million NFT units will be issued in the financing."
  • Inomin intends to allocate the gross proceeds raised from the sale of the NFT Units as follows: 50% for general working capital; 35% to pay non-arm's length management and geological service fees; 10% for investor relations activities; and 5% for property evaluations.
  • Source: Press Release
